content.full_text:
RAy! MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 7, 1997
Ronald R. Ball
City Attorney
City of Carlsbad
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad, California 92008-1989
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-192
DearMr. Ball:
This letter is a response to your request on behalfofMr. Evan Becker for advice
regarding the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
Does Mr. Becker have a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to governmental
decisions about permit applications filed by Mr. Avis?
CONCLUSION
Mr. Becker appears to have a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to
governmental decisions about permit applications filed by Mr. Avis. Mr. Becker may not make,
participate in making, or use or attempt to use his official position to influence these
governmental decisions.
FACTS
Mr. Becker is the Housing and Redevelopment Director for the City of Carlsbad. He
makes final decisions on administrative permits in the Redevelopment Area for the city, subject
to appeal to the city’s Design Review Board. He also makes recommendations to the city’s
Housing Commission and to the city council.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660
--


File No. A-97-192
Page 2
Mr. Becker is a friend of Mr. Doug Avis, a principal in a local real estate development
company. In the course ofhis business, Mr. Avis processes applications through various city
departments, including the Housing and Redevelopment Department.
Messrs. Becker and Avis have fished together many times, sharing the expenses for
boats, gas, bait, etc. In September 1996, Mr. Becker purchased a fishing boat. He is the sole
owner of the boat subject to a note to a major bank on which he is the sole borrower. Mr. Becker
and Mr. Avis have an informal agreement that as long they fish together as they have in the past,
Mr. Avis would share costs and expenses, including a payment of $125 per month (which is
about one half ofthe boat payment) toward those costs and expenses.
ANALYSIS
The purpose of the Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that public officials
will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests ofpersons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to “public officials.” “Public
official,” for purposes of the Act, is defined to include every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local agency (with certain exceptions not relevant here). (Section 82048;
Regulation 18700.) As Housing and Redevelopment Director, Mr. Becker is a public official for
purposes of the Act.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only tofinancial conflicts. A public
official’s financial interests presents a disqualiQ,iing conflict of interest if it is reasonably
foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a materialfinancial effect on the interest.
“Financial interest” is defined, for purposes ofthe Act, in Section 87103. In essence, Section
87103 covers six kinds of financial interests (see next paragraph). For purposes of the Act
“reasonably foreseeable” means a substantial likelihood that a financial effect will occur. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether a financial effect is material is determined under
various regulations promulgated by the FPPC, depending upon the nature ofthe interest and the
degree to which it is involved. (Regulation 18700 et seq.)
Five of the six kinds of financial interests regulated by the Act are enumerated in
subdivisions (a)-(e) of Section 87103: (a) a business entity in which the public official has an
investment of $1,000 or more; (b)real property in which the public official has an interest of
$1,000 or more; (c) any source of income which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision; (d) a business entity in which the public official is an officer, director,
manager, etc.; and, (e) the donor of gifts to the public official ifthe gifts aggregate to $250 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision. Finally, the public official has a financial interest if
the governmental decision will have a “personal effect” on him/her or his/her immediate family,


File No. A-97-192
Page 3
whether positive or negative, of at least $250 in any 12-month period. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18702.1 (a)(4).)
Mr. Becker receives a payment of $125 per month from Mr. Avis. This payment is not a
gift because Mr. Becker provides consideration of equal value to Mr. Avis, in the form of the use
of the boat. (Section 82028.) However, the monthly payment is income to Mr. Becker, and
Mr. Avis is the source of that income. (Section 82030(a).) One of the six kinds of financial
interests covered by the Act’s conflict-of-interest rules is “any source of income which
aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months.” (Section 87103(c).) Therefore, Mr. Avis is a
“source of income” to Mr. Becker, within the meaning of the Act’s conflict-of-interest rules.
As Housing and Redevelopment Director, Mr. Becker may not make, participate in
making, or use or attempt to use his position to influence any governmental decision which has a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his source of income. (Section 87100.) As a
consequence ofhis business, Mr. Avis processes applications through various city agencies,
including Mr. Becker’s department. Mr. Becker appears to be in a position to make
governmental decisions with regard to these applications because he has final decision-making
authority on certain administrative permits.
2 Mr. Becker also appears to be in a position to
participate in making governmental decisions about these applications because he makes
recommendations to the Housing Commission and the city council.
3
Mr. Becker almost
certainly is in a position to use his official position to influence or attempt to influence decisions
2 A public official “makes a governmental decision,” when the official, acting within the authority of his
or her office or position, does any of the following:
•
votes on a matter,
•
appoints a person,
•
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action,
—
•
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency,
•
or determines not to do anything of these things, unless such determination is made because ofhis or her
fmancial interest. (Regulation 18700(b).)
A public official “participates in making a governmental decision,” when, acting within the authority of
his or her position, the official does any of the following:
•
negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding a
governmental decision,
•
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, by conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision, or by
preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally, or in writing, which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(c).)


File No. A-97-192
Page 4
about Mr. Avis’ permit applications within the department.
4
Mr. Avis is directly involved in the permit applications because he “[i]nitiates the
proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application ...“ for the permits.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).) As a directly involved source of income, the reasonably foreseeable
financial effects of the decision are considered to be material. (Regulation 18702.1(a).)
Therefore, we conclude that Mr. Becker has a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to
permit applications filed by Mr. Avis.
5 He may not make, participate in making or use his
official position to influence governmental decisions about these applications.
Ifyou have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. ChurchwLI
e
a C/unsel
/
y: John Vgelli
Staff Cbunsel, Legal Division
SGC:JV:ak
When the relevant governmental decision is within or before the public official’s own agency, or an
agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official’s agency, “... the official is attempting
to use his or her official position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a
business entity, client, or customer.” (Regulation 18700.1(a).)
Given the presumably unique nature of each permit application, the exception to the conflict of interest
rules for decisions which have a fmancial effect on the public official’s source of income which is indistinguishable
from the fmancial effect on the public generally does not apply. (See Regulation 18703 et seq.)

participate in making, or use or attempt to use his official position to influence these
governmental decisions.

QUESTION: Does Mr. Becker have a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to governmental
decisions about permit applications filed by Mr. Avis?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Becker appears to have a disqualifying conflict of interest with regard to
governmental decisions about permit applications filed by Mr. Avis. Mr. Becker may not make,
participate in making, or use or attempt to use his official position to influence these
governmental decisions.
